---
title: 'Hands-on Introduction to R^[`r library(r2symbols) ; format(paste(symbol("copyright") , " - Wim R.M. Cardoen, 2022 - The content can neither be copied nor distributed without the **explicit** permission of the author."))`]'
subtitle: 'Section 5: Heterogeneous vectors (Lists & Dataframes) and IO'
author: "Wim R.M. Cardoen"
date: "Last updated: `r format(Sys.time(), ' %2m/%2d/%Y @ %2H:%2M:%2S')`"
output:
  html_document:
    highlight: tango
    df_print: tibble
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 5
    number_sections: true
    mathjax: default
lang: en
---

In the first part of this section, two kinds^[R also has the pairlist. This topic will not be discussed in this section. People interested in this subject should have a look at [R-internals](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-ints.pdf).] of **heterogeneous** vectors will be discussed:

* lists
* data frames (& tibbles)

**Input-output (IO)** in R forms the subject of the latter part.

# R Lists

A `list` is a vector that **may** contain one or more **components**.
The components can be **heterogeneous** objects (atomic types, functions, lists^[Due to this feature, they are also called recursive vectors.], ...).

Under the hood, the list is implemented as a vector of pointers to its top-level components.
The list's length equals the number of top-level components.

## Creation of a list

An R list can be created in several ways:

* using the `list()` function (most common)
* via the `vector()` function
* via a cast using the `as.list()` function

### Examples

* use of the `list()` function

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  # Creating an empty list
  x1 <- list()
  str(x1)
  ```

  ```{R, echo=FALSE, comment=''}
  cat(sprintf("typeof(x1):%s   class(x1):%s   length(x1):%d\n", 
                 typeof(x1), class(x1), length(x1)))
  ```

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  # A more realistic list
  x2 <- list(1:10, c("hello","world"), 
             3+4i, matrix(data=1:6,nrow=2,ncol=3,byrow=TRUE))
  str(x2)
  ```

  ```{R, echo=FALSE, comment=''}
  cat(sprintf("typeof(x2):%s   class(x2):%s   length(x2):%d\n", 
                 typeof(x2), class(x2), length(x2)))
  ```

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  # Using existing names
  x3 <- list(x=1, y=2, str1="hello", str2="world", vec=1:5)
  str(x3)
  ```

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  # Applying names to a list
  x4 <- list(matrix(data=1:4,nrow=2,ncol=2), c(T,F,T,T), "hello")
  names(x4) <- c("mymat","mybool","mystr")
  str(x4)
  ```

* use `vector()` function:

  Allows to create/allocate an empty vector of a certain length.

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  # Allocate a vector of length 5
  x5 <- vector(mode="list", length=5)
  str(x5)
  ```

* using the `as.list()` function

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  x6 <- as.list(matrix(5:10,nrow=2))
  str(x6)
  ```

  Note: The inverse operation is `unlist()`

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  x7 <- unlist(x6)
  str(x7)
  ```

## Accessor operators `[]`, `[[]]`, `$` in R

### General statements

The operator `[[i]]` selects **only one component** (in cases of lists) or
**only one element** in case of homogeneous vectors.

The operator `[]` allows to select **one or more components** (in the case of lists)
or **one or more elements** in the case of homogeneous vectors.

The `$` operator can **only** be used for **generic/recursive vectors**.
If you use the `$` operator on other objects you will obtain an **error**.
The `$` operator can **only** be followed by a string or a non-computable index.

### Homogeneous vectors

In practice, for homogeneous vectors there is hardly any difference between `[[]]` and `[]`
**except** that `[[]]` does **NOT** allow to select more than **one** element.

**Note: The operator `[[]]` can be used as a tool of defensive programming.**


#### Examples

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
a <- seq(from=1,to=30,by=3)
a
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extraction of ONE element
cat(sprintf(" a[[2]] : %d\n", a[[2]]))
cat(sprintf(" a[2]   : %d\n", a[2]))
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, error=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extraction of MORE than 1 element using [[]] => ERROR
a[[c(2,3)]]
```

**but:**
```{R, echo=TRUE, error=FALSE, comment=''}
# Extraction of MORE than 1 element using [] => OK
a[c(2,3)]
```

### Heterogeneous vectors (i.e. lists and derived classes)

We stated earlier that the operator `[[]]` allows to select **only one** component.
It also means that this operator selects **the component as is** (matrix, list, function, ...).

The operator `[]` allows to select more than **one** component.
Therefore, in order to return potentially heterogeneous components it **always**
returns a `list` even if only one component were to be returned.

#### Examples

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
str(x2)
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, error=FALSE, comment=''}
# Selection using [[]]
x24 <- x2[[4]]
x24
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
class(x24)
typeof(x24)
length(x24)
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, error=FALSE, comment=''}
# Selection using []
x24 <- x2[4]
x24
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
class(x24)
typeof(x24)
length(x24)
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Select third el. of the FIRST component
x13 <- x2[[1]][3] 
x13
```

which is the same as:

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
v1 <- x2[[1]] 
v1[3]
```

**Heterogeneous** vectors are also known as **recursive/generic** vectors,
as can be seen in the following example:

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# A more advanced 'recursive' example
v <- list(v1=1:4, 
          lst1=list(a=3, b=2, c=list(x=5,y=7, v2=seq(from=7,to=12))))
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extracting the component as a homogeneous vector
v[[2]][[3]][[3]]
class(v[[2]][[3]][[3]])
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extracting as a list
v[[2]][[3]][3]
class(v[[2]][[3]][3])
```

We can extract the same data using names if available:

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
v$lst1$c$v2
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# List of function objects
lstfunc <- list(cube=function(x){x**3},
                quartic=function(x){x**4})
lstfunc$cube(5)
lstfunc$quartic(5)
```

### Exercises

* Let's consider the following list:
  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  lstex1 <- list(
                 list(
                      seq(from=4,to=40,by=5),
                      "hello world",
                      list(
                           matrix(100:119,nrow=5),
                           "bye",
                           c(7,13,17),
                           list(451,-1,-17) 
                          )
                     )
                )
  ```

  Extract the following data from `lstex1`:

  * the elements $7\,13$ as a vector.
  * the second column of `matrix(100:119,nrow=5)` as a matrix.
  * $-1$ as a scalar.
  * $-1$ as a list.
  * **all** numerical values into a vector. (Hint: `unlist()`)


## Modifying lists

* **Removal/deletion** of components:

  Set the list element which refers to the component to `NULL`.
  The list will be **automatically** re-indexed and its length adjusted.

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  mylst1 <- list(a=1:10, b=seq(1,5), matrix(1:10, nrow=2), 
                "hello", "world", 1:5 )
  str(mylst1)              
  ```

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  # Removal of the 5th component
  mylst1[[5]] <- NULL
  str(mylst1)
  ```

* **Appending** an object:

  Assign the object (`obj`) to the list element with index `length(lst)+1`

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  # Creation of a list mylst2
  mylst2 <- list( 1:5, 'a' , 'b')
  str(mylst2)
  ```

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, comment=''}
  # Appending a Boolean vector to the existing list mylst2
  mylst2[[length(mylst2)+1]] <- c(T,F,T)
  str(mylst2)
  ```

  If you set the index to a number which is **larger** than `length(lst)+1`
  all the new intermittent list elements will be set to `NULL`.
  You can get rid of these additional `NULL` values by **subsequently** deleting them.

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, comment=''}
  # Insert a component at an index > length(mylst2)+1
  # -> we will get some intermittent NULL values.
  mylst2[[7]] <- "value"
  str(mylst2)
  ```

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, comment=''}
  # Delete the NULL values! Start from the end!
  mylst2[[6]] <- NULL
  mylst2[[5]] <- NULL
  str(mylst2)
  ```

* **Insertion** of new components

  Create a new vector containing three parts:
  * the 'left' sublist
  * the new components
  * the 'right' sublist

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, comment=''}
  str(mylst2) 
  ```

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, comment=''}
  # Add new component at index 4
  newlst2 <- c(mylst2[1:3], "NEW", mylst2[4:length(mylst2)])
  str(newlst2)
  ```

### Exercises

* Create the following list:
  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  lstex2 <- list(1:10,
                 matrix(seq(from=1,to=20), nrow=4),
                 5+3i,
                 list('a','d','b'),
                 "UoU",
                 c(T,F,T,T,T),
                 "Hello"
                )
  ```

  Perform some operations (deletions, insertions, modifications) on `lstex2`
  such that `lstex2` takes on the following form:

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  lstex2 <- list( matrix(seq(from=1,to=20), nrow=4),
                 "Hello UoU",
                 list('a','b','c','d'),
                 c(T,F,T,T,T)
                )
  ```


## Functions: return multiple objects

If a function needs to return **multiple objects** a list **must** be used.

### Example

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
func01 <- function(n)
{
   x <- n*(n+1)/2
   y <- cbind(1:n,(1:n)^2)
   return(list('x'=x,'y'=y))
} 
n <- 8
res <- func01(n)
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
res$x
res$y
```

### Exercises

* Write the function `countOcc(content)` which returns

  * the occurrence of each letter in the string `content`.
  * the occurrence of each non-letter character in the string `content`.

  ```{R, echo=FALSE}
  # Auxiliary function to count the occurrence of each character in the vec
  countAux <- function(vec){

     uniq_el <- unique(vec) 
   
     if (length(uniq_el)>0) {
         count <- vector(mode="integer", length=length(uniq_el)) 
         for(i in seq_along(uniq_el)){
             count[i] <- sum(uniq_el[i] == vec)
         }
         names(count) <- uniq_el
         return(count)
     }
     return(NULL)
  }   

  # Count the occurrence of 
  #   a. the letters in content
  #   b. the non-letters in content
  countOccurrence <- function(content){
  
     vec <- strsplit(content, split='')
     vec <- tolower(vec[[1]])
     vecLetters <- sort(vec[vec %in% letters])
     cvecLetters <- vec[!(vec %in% letters)]
   
     return(list(  countAlpha=countAux(vecLetters),
                 countNonAlpha=countAux(cvecLetters)))
  }   
  ```

  If we use the text of the First Amendment of the Bill of Rights^[The text was obtained from <https://www.law.cornell.edu/constitution/first_amendment>.] as argument for `countOcc`,

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
    # Split the text to make it readable
    l1 <- "Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion,"
    l2 <- "or prohibiting the free exercise thereof;"
    l3 <- "or abridging the freedom of speech, or of the press;"
    l4 <- "or the right of the people peaceably to assemble,"
    l5 <- "and to petition the government for a redress of grievances."
    # Glue the chunks {l1,l2,l3,l4,l5} together
    firstamend <- paste(l1, l2, l3, l4, l5, sep=" ")
  ```

  we obtain the following output:

  ```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
  res <- countOccurrence(firstamend)
  res
  ```

  Note/hints:

  * We don't distinguish lowercase letters from uppercase letters.
  * `strsplit()`: splits a string into its characters.
  * `tolower()`: converts letters to their lower case counterparts.
  * `unique()`: extracts the unique elements of a vector.
  * R has a built-in vector `letters`.

---

# R Dataframes

A `data frame` is a `list` with three `attributes`:

* `names`: component names
* `row.names`: row names
* `class`: `data.frame`

From the above, we can infer that a data frame has the same row names for each component (columns).
The components of a data frame can be vectors, factors, numerical matrices, lists or other data frames.

In practice, a **data frame** can be conceptualized as a **generalized (i.e. heterogeneous) matrix/table**
**where each column has its own type but where each column has the same number of rows**.

## Creating a data frame

* use of the `data.frame` function.

* some IO functions generate a data frame when they read a file,
  e.g. `read.table()`, `read.csv()`.

### Examples

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Creation of a data frame using the data.frame function:
vec1 <- c("Smith", "Jensen", "McFall", "Johnson", 
          "Brown", "Williams", "Wilson", "Roberts")
vec2 <- c(4, 3, 0 , 6, 2, 0, 5, 1)
vec3 <- c(100000, 80000, 140000, 120000, 60000, 30000, 170000, 100000)
vec4 <- c("Salt Lake", "Provo", "Park City", "Provo", 
          "Logan", "Ogden", "Provo", "Salt Lake")
df1  <- data.frame(family=vec1, nchildren=vec2, income=vec3, location=vec4)
df1
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Creation of a data frame after reading a data file
df2 <- read.table(file="./data/seaice.txt", header=TRUE)
cat(sprintf("Length(df2) :%d\n", length(df2)))
cat(sprintf("Dim. of df2 :\n"))
dim(df2)
```

**Note**:

* Since R 4.0.0, the default value for the argument `stringsAsFactors` in the function
  `data.frame()` has been set to `FALSE`.

* The functions `head()` and `tail()` display the first $n$, respectively last $n$ lines
  (default: 6) of a data frame.

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Head of data frame df2 with the first 6 lines (default)
head(df2)
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Tail of data frame df2 with the last 4 lines
tail(df2, n=4)
```

## Accessing elements of a data frame

As we discussed previously, a data frame is a list with some extra attributes.
But it also has features of a (heterogeneous) matrix.

Therefore, the elements of a data frame can be accessed in **two** different ways:

* using the list syntax
* using the matrix syntax

### Examples

* Using the `list` syntax:

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extract the names of the components (columns)
names(df1)
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extract the list's second component as a vector
str(df1[[2]])
cat(sprintf("df1[[2]]:%s\n", typeof(df1[[2]])))
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extract the list's second component as a list (dataframe)
df1[2]
str(df1[2])
cat(sprintf("df1[2]:%s\n", typeof(df1[2])))
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extract a list's component using its name
df1$location
str(df1$location)
```

* Using the `matrix` syntax:

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
colnames(df1)
rownames(df1)
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extract a few elements
df1[3,3]
df1[8,4]
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extract a column => vector
df1[, 3]
df1[,'income']
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extract a column but preserve a dataframe
df1[,'income',drop=FALSE]
typeof(df1[,'income',drop=FALSE])
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Extract everything except fourth column
df1[c(1,4,5),-4]
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Find all the families with an income >=100,000
ind <- which(df1$income >= 100000)
df1[ind,'family']
```

## Modifying the data frame

* Adding columns and rows: use the `cbind()` and `rbind()` functions (as in the case of matrices)

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Create a new data frame
v1 <- 1:10
v2 <- 11:20
v3 <- 21:30
mydf1 <- data.frame(x=v1, y=v2, z=v3)
mydf1
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Add a new column
mydf2 <- cbind(mydf1, w=31:40)
mydf2
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Add a new row
mydf3 <- rbind(mydf2, c(11,21,31,41))
mydf3
```

* Remove columns and rows:

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Remove columns 
mydf3$z <- NULL      # Syntax 1
mydf3[,2] <- NULL    # Syntax 2
mydf3
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, comment=''}
# Remove row
mydf3 <- mydf3[-c(4,7,8),]
mydf3
```


## Attach and detach operations

* `attach()`: allows to use a data frame's variables without invoking the name of the data frame,
  i.e. inserts the data frame on the **search path**.

* `detach()`: undoes the `attach()` operation but can also unload a library.

* `search()`: gives an array of the **attached** packages and R objects (usually data frames).

This topic will be discussed in more detail in a future section (Environments).

### Examples

* Use of the `attach()` and `search()` functions:

```{R, echo=TRUE, error=TRUE, comment=''}
# Prior to attaching mydf1
mydf1
search()
x
```

```{R, echo=TRUE, error=TRUE, comment=''}
# attach the data frame
attach(mydf1)
search()
x
```

* Use of the `detach()` function:

```{R, echo=TRUE, error=TRUE, comment=''}
# detach the data frame
detach(mydf1)
search()
x
```

## Exercises

The R package contains a lot of data sets. We will use
the `airquality` data set in the subsequent exercise.

* Return the first 5 and the last 2 entries of the data frame.
  You can load the data frame by invoking `airquality` on the command line.
* How many Ozone entries are missing in the data frame?
* Find the day and month when the max. temperature occurred.
* What is the mean & standard deviation of the Ozone level
  for the days when the temperature >= 80?
* How many days have a Solar.R between 150 and 250?

---

# Input-Output (IO)

## Functionality in Base R

## Other options

* library [readr](https://readr.tidyverse.org/)
  - supports a lot of formats (csv, tcsv, delim, ...)
  - allows column specification
  - faster than Base R's read/write operations
  - uses a `tibble` instead of a `data frame`
  - for more info: [R for Data Science - Chapter 11: Data import](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/data-import.html)

* library [data.table](https://github.com/Rdatatable/data.table)
  - very fast IO: optimal for large read (`fread()`) and write (`fwrite()`) operations
  - memory efficient
  - low-level parallelism (use of multiple CPU threads)
